THE COURT ADOPTED THE TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER IS SUSTAINED WITH PREJUDICE; WORKER'S COMPENSATION WAS PLAINTIFF'S EXCLUSIVE REMEDY. COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING AND THAT COMPLIES WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER. CLERK: JACQUELINE ALAMEDA. DEPUTY: MALONE. MATTER NOT REPORTED. (302/RBU)

calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-23-2024 Off Calendar

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Jun-20-2024 Continued To Sep-18-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-06-2024 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Geoffrey Kasler, Esq., Tel. 310.289.9100, appearing via Zoom for plaintiff Krystal Rquesl Roberts;
Katherine B. Bearman, Esq., Tel. 415.554.4256, appearing in person for defendant City and County of San Francisco.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

The matter is contested late by plaintiff. Brief discussions are held.

The Court allows argument. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer is sustained with prejudice; worker's compensation was plaintiff's exclusive remedy.

High on boilerplate, the check-box complaint is low on detail. But combining the pled facts with those judicially noticeable shows plaintiff was employed as a City parking control officer when injured on Portola Drive while "midnight street cleaning" was performed. (Cmplt.; RJN 1-3.) Straining her right wrist and leg, plaintiff went on "temporary total disability" for 17 months, collecting $75,711.30 in worker's compensation benefits. (Id.)

Plaintiff concedes "it is true that worker's compensation may be the exclusive remedy for an employee's injury that occurs during employment," but invokes a "dual capacity doctrine." (Opp. 6:29-7:2.) The argument has two fatal flaws. First, the cases relied on are nothing like ours: Miller v. King (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 1732 [waitress injured on job sues non-employer owners of property where restaurant located]; Weinstein v. St. Mary's Medical Center (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 1223 [nurse injured on hospital property while not working].) Second, plaintiff says she "worked for" San Francisco Municipal Railway, but "has sued" the City. (Opp. 11:13.) However, the former is a department of the latter; they are not separate legal entities.

The parties' other arguments need not be reached.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Deputy: Malone. Matter not reported. (302/RBU)

Atty. KATHERINE BEARMAN, Esq. of the Office of the City Attorney (tel: 415-554-4256) for Defendant.
